critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with genuine attention to common failure modes. I'll work through the issues I can identify, organized by root cause.

---

**Root Cause 1: Temporal leakage through the stratified split design**

The methodology explicitly uses incident-density-stratified sampling over the full 24-month window, mixing events from all time periods across train/validation/test. This is a subtle but serious problem.

The positive labels are assigned to events within 72 hours of a confirmed incident. Incident confirmation is a downstream, post-hoc process — forensics investigations, customer reports, password resets — that can take days to weeks after the events themselves. When the split intermixes time periods, training examples from month 18 can sit adjacent to test examples from month 6. More critically, the behavior-sequence features (7-day rolling login stats, 14-day distinct IP counts, 30-day email volumes) computed for a month-18 event will look into a time window that overlaps with events that may appear in the test set. Even if the split is done at the event level and the raw logs are partitioned, the rolling features computed at extraction time will have incorporated cross-split signal if the feature computation is not properly bounded to the split's temporal extent.

The specific claim: stratified non-overlapping sampling over a 24-month corpus produces valid train/test separation.
The failure mechanism: rolling behavioral features, computed before splitting, encode information from the entire 24-month window into every event. An event in the 'training' split has its 30-day rolling window computed using logs from what the test split will contain. This is not label leakage but distributional leakage — the features for test events incorporate statistics from future production that were only available because the data was extracted retrospectively.
Evidence: Compare model PR-AUC on a strict temporal holdout (train on months 1–17, test on months 18–24) against the stratified holdout performance. If the stratified holdout materially outperforms temporal holdout, leakage is the likely explanation. Also check whether the rolling-feature computation explicitly masks future data relative to each event's timestamp.

---

**Root Cause 2: Label construction conflates detection signal with incident-response workflow artifacts**

The positive label is 'any event within 72 hours of a confirmed incident.' Confirmed incidents are identified through forensic investigation, password resets, or customer reports. These investigation processes themselves generate security events — analyst logins, alert system queries, internal ticketing activity. If any of these artifacts appear in the authentication or email metadata logs, events that are positive-labeled because they co-occur with the response (not the attack) will carry features that encode 'incident response is happening,' not 'account is compromised.'

The methodology does mention a negative control (shuffle labels temporally) but that control addresses spurious time trends, not response-workflow confounds. The response workflow confound is localized to the 72-hour window, not a smooth temporal trend, so the negative control would not detect it.

Further, 'confirmed account takeover' as a label source creates a selection effect: only incidents that were eventually confirmed appear as positives. Attacks that were missed entirely by the incident response team are labeled negative. The model therefore learns to predict 'this attack resembles ones we already know how to catch,' which is not the same as 'this account is compromised.' This is a fundamental ceiling on recall that the methodology does not acknowledge.

The specific claim: positive labels identify true compromise events.
The failure mechanism: (a) response-workflow events co-occur with positives and carry distinctive features; (b) only discovered incidents are labeled positive, so the model learns the signature of detectable rather than actual compromise.
Evidence: (a) Audit the feature values of positively-labeled events in the 0–6 hour post-incident window vs. the 6–72 hour window — if analyst/response activities pollute the feature space, the early window will have different feature distributions. (b) Cross-reference model false negatives with any retrospectively discovered incidents to quantify selection bias in label coverage.

---

**Root Cause 3: Baseline re-tuning on the validation set creates an artificially favorable ML comparison**

The methodology re-tunes the rules-based baseline on the validation set using the same features as the ML model, including pre-computed deviation scores that the rules 'cannot natively compute.' This is correct in principle — you want the best possible baseline — but it introduces a structural asymmetry: the rules-based system was designed to operate without ML-derived features. Providing those features to the rules system and re-tuning it on validation converts it into a hybrid that no longer represents the production baseline. The production baseline presumably doesn't have access to those features.

The comparison then answers: 'does XGBoost beat an optimally re-tuned rules system that has already been augmented with ML-derived features?' rather than 'does the ML pipeline beat the current production system?' If the augmented rules system with re-tuned thresholds already captures most of the signal, the ML model's incremental gain may be marginal and not operationally meaningful.

The specific claim: the re-tuned baseline is a fair comparison point.
The failure mechanism: the re-tuned baseline is no longer the production system — it has been given features it doesn't use in production and optimized on validation data. The gain attributed to ML may actually be the gain from the feature engineering, which would also improve the rules system. Disentangling these is necessary to know what is actually being bought by adopting gradient boosting.
Evidence: Report a three-way comparison: (1) production baseline as-is, (2) production baseline re-tuned on validation with its original features only, (3) production baseline re-tuned with augmented features, (4) ML model. If gap between (3) and (4) is small while gap between (1) and (3) is large, the feature engineering is the story, not the model class.

---

**Root Cause 4: The 'operating-point confirmation' on the test set constitutes a second look**

Section 6 states: 'the held-out test set will be used for a final operating-point confirmation to ensure the selected score threshold and alert budget remain well aligned with the organization's current incident load before freezing the comparison. Final precision-recall AUC and secondary metrics will then be reported on that same held-out set.'

This is a form of double-dipping. The test set is used first to confirm the operating point, and then to report final metrics. Even if the PR-AUC is threshold-agnostic, the secondary metrics (recall@90% precision, precision@80% recall) depend on the threshold. If the threshold is adjusted after seeing the test set distribution — even informally for 'alignment' purposes — the secondary metrics are no longer held-out estimates. The phrase 'before freezing the comparison' implies looking at test performance before reporting it.

The specific claim: test set metrics are uncontaminated holdout estimates.
The failure mechanism: using the test set for operating-point confirmation, even without explicit optimization, creates an opportunity for implicit threshold adjustment that inflates secondary metric performance.
Evidence: The protocol should specify that the threshold is set on the validation set without reference to test outcomes, and the test set is evaluated exactly once. If the operating-point confirmation requires seeing test set label distributions, it is contamination.

---

**Summary of genuine issues:**

1. Temporal/feature leakage via rolling features computed across the full corpus before stratified splitting — this is the most serious issue and could materially inflate reported performance.
2. Label construction creates a selection effect (only discovered incidents are positive) and a response-workflow confound (response events co-occur with positives) — these are not addressed by the existing confound controls.
3. Baseline augmentation with ML-derived features conflates model-class contribution with feature-engineering contribution.
4. Test set double-use for operating-point confirmation contaminates secondary metrics.

Issues 1 and 4 are methodological flaws with clear, mechanistic failure modes. Issues 2 and 3 are real but could be acknowledged as scope limitations rather than invalidating flaws — the methodology's own scope statement partially covers them.
all_issues_raised: Rolling behavioral features (7/14/30-day windows) computed over the full 24-month corpus before stratified splitting will encode cross-split signal, constituting distributional leakage even if event-level partitioning is otherwise correct.

Positive labels identify only discovered incidents, not all compromises, so the model learns the signature of detectable attacks rather than actual compromise — a selection bias the negative control does not address.

Response-workflow events (analyst logins, alert system activity) co-occur with positive-labeled events within the 72-hour window and may carry distinctive features that the model exploits as compromise signals.

Re-tuning the rules baseline on the validation set with augmented ML-derived features converts it into a hybrid system that doesn't represent the production baseline, conflating feature-engineering gains with model-class gains.
Using the test set for 'operating-point confirmation' before reporting secondary metrics constitutes a second look that can inflate threshold-dependent secondary metrics.

Re-tuning the rules baseline on the validation set with augmented ML-derived features converts it into a hybrid system that doesn't represent the production baseline, conflating feature-engineering gains with model-class gains.
Using the test set for 'operating-point confirmation' before reporting secondary metrics constitutes a second look that can inflate threshold-dependent secondary metrics.
